point_001: The method is only evaluated on a single, proprietary 540B parameter model (PaLM), making it impossible to determine whether self-improvement is a general property of large language models or specific to this one model and scale.
point_002: The results are not reproducible by the broader research community because PaLM is not publicly available and the computational resources required are inaccessible to most labs, universities, and companies.
point_003: The approach has not been tested on smaller, publicly available models (e.g., under 10B parameters), leaving open the question of whether the method generalizes to more accessible model sizes.
point_004: The novelty of the method is questionable, as it primarily combines existing techniques—chain-of-thought prompting, self-consistency, and fine-tuning on self-generated data—without introducing fundamentally new ideas; the contribution is largely in execution rather than ideation.
point_005: The paper does not adequately explain why the proposed approach works theoretically; it lacks a principled analysis of the mechanism behind self-improvement beyond empirical demonstration.
point_006: A key baseline is missing: the paper does not compare LMSI against self-consistency with a larger number of samples (without fine-tuning), which could potentially match the performance gains attributed to the self-improvement fine-tuning step.
point_007: The claim that the approach resembles how the human brain learns is made without any theoretical grounding, cognitive science evidence, or supporting study, making it an unsupported analogy.
point_008: The paper does not sufficiently discuss whether self-improvement is inherently a property of very large models, and whether smaller models with lower baseline accuracy would fail because their self-generated outputs are too noisy to be useful for fine-tuning.
point_009: Some ablation results (e.g., Figure 3, where step-by-step and few-shot with step-by-step curves converge at 80 total samples) are presented without adequate discussion or explanation of the observed phenomena.
